comment.body:
its more about the mindset, to analyse the market. not the risk management directly.
risk management is a bit different in market space... 
but you know, if you go to vegas, you have to accept first that the maffia don't want you to take their money. the house wants your money.
they cheat, they always do. they manipulate the games, the bets etc. to their own favor. (Michael Franzese, ex-capo regime has a good story about it how it was done under him with a baseball player)

and if you wanna win, you have to learn to think with their head. recognizing when the odds are over or underpriced, and what side they want you to push in... and you bet against it, with prober sizing (like Kelly. thats the best)

comment.body:
is that you have to learn to think with their heads.
you get the common cards on the table. 
you watch the people around you how they reacted in the first 2-3 rounds.
you know your cards, (but he was like didn't even watched them, but took the bets before... he has read so good the plp), and then you make assumption of the probabilities of their cards.
you watch how they react, and it helps you to assume what they likely have in their hand.
and so you can decide how much you should bet on that.

comment.body:
@Jeffrey K for your yesterday question.
schematically, speaking:
think about gamma as the amount dealers have to trade when spot moves by one unit.
if you are long lets say 10 gamma,
if spot moves up, you have to sell 10 shares of stocks to adjust your initial hedge (doesn't matter if you hedge call or put, the ending result is the same)

okay, now we now that gamma changes relative to spot (called "speed"). 
so lets say one unit higher you only long 8 gamma
means, if spot moves up, you will still sell against the price move, but only 8 shares of stocks this time.

on the next lvl, you only long 5 gamma
again, if spot moves up, you short sell only 5 shares of stocks. and so on...
so price has less and less resistance from gamma based delta hedging, and it lets the range of returns expand slowly to the upside, causing RV to expand a bit.

now, if it reaches zero gamma line, then there is no gamma impact on that lvl when spot moves up or down, but then it flips to negative.
And when you have negative gamma, lets say you short 5 gamma, if spot moves up, you have to BUY 5 more shares of stock. buying this time.

quick math: 
if you are long ATM put: you have -50 delta, you long 50 shares of stock.
in positive gamma exposure, lets say you long 2 gamma, if spot moves up by one unit, you will have -50 + 2 = -48 delta to hedge, you only need to hedge 48 delta, so you sell 2 shares of stock
now, in negative gamma exposure, if you would have short that 2 gamma, if spot moves up by one unit, you would have -50 + -2 = -52, so you would have buy 2 more shares of stocks.

if you are long ATM call: you long 50 delta, you short 50 shares of stock,
long 2 gamma, spot up: 50 + 2 = 52, sell 2 shares of stock
short 2 gamma, spot up: 50 -2 = 48, buy back 2 shares of stock.

same thing happens with calls and puts

so guys, this what i just explained here, is the primal key to understand the markets, and frontrun it.
second is the anomalies: momentum, mean reversion, mostly (read my post about efficient market, there i explained the most important ones)

so like in poker: first you check the odds, how they move, 
second, you check the reactions, the patterns,
third, you check the cards.

comment.body:
and this is why i didn't start to post about greeks and all of these 'fancy stuffs" yet, bcs it's not the most important thing. 
it is important if you are a market maker, otherwise it is secondary.
i know a some good traders who don't even understand options market, and have 60%+ win rate. all they watch is momentum, mean reversion, and good risk management, and intuition for sentiment.

I don't like when somebody wants to explain everything through positioning, bcs thats just not how it works. i showed a simple example above, with the risk reversal.
And if you understand what i dropped in my educational posts, you will get the options market significance. it is huge, but different from how some interprete it, overemphatizing it, like if this would be the only driver of the market... 
no. its customer sentiment. 
the supply/demand ratio for VOLATILITY

comment.body:
Hi Alma~
Always appreciate your insights, and hope you enjoy the pole dancing;)
I have two questions about your vol related posts yesterday.

1. Why do MMs hedge vol?
What's the purpose of setting a vol target / why do they need a multiplier to scale the portfolio exposure? Hedging delta and gamma is easier to understand since they directly impact PnL, but I struggle to understand what hedge volatility achieve.

2. Predict or observe?
When you analyze the market, do you try to figure out MMs' targets like "today they are focusing on delta-neutral because of XXX", or is it impossible to know, so we should just observe various greek flows and find trading opportunities like yesterday's gap between vol-of-vol and RV?
comment.raw_body:
Hi Alma~
Always appreciate your insights, and hope you enjoy the pole dancing;)
I have two questions about your vol related posts yesterday.

1. Why do MMs hedge vol?
What's the purpose of setting a vol target / why do they need a multiplier to scale the portfolio exposure? Hedging delta and gamma is easier to understand since they directly impact PnL, but I struggle to understand what hedge volatility achieve.

2. Predict or observe?
When you analyze the market, do you try to figure out MMs' targets like "today they are focusing on delta-neutral because of XXX", or is it impossible to know, so we should just observe various greek flows and find trading opportunities like yesterday's gap between vol-of-vol and RV?
